elements.sent-2411-001: Decades ago, Sondheimer discovered that the electric conductivity of metallic crystals hosting ballistic electrons oscillates with magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-002: These oscillations, periodic in magnetic field and the period proportional to the sample thickness, have been understood in a semi-classical framework.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here, we present a study of longitudinal and transverse conductivity in cadmium single crystals with thickness varying between 12.6 to 475 $\mu$m.
elements.sent-2411-004: When the magnetic field is sufficiently large or the sample sufficiently thick, the amplitude of oscillation falls off as $B^{-4}$ as previously reported.
elements.sent-2411-005: In contrast, the ten first oscillations follow a $B^{-2.5 e^{-B/B_0}$ field dependence and their amplitude is set by the quantum of conductance, the sample thickness, the magnetic length and the Fermi surface geometry.}
elements.sent-2411-006: We demonstrate that they are beyond the semi-classical picture, as the exponential prefactor indicates quantum tunneling between distinct quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-007: We draw a picture of these quantum oscillations, in which the linear dispersion of the semi-Dirac band in the cadmium plays a crucial role.
elements.sent-2411-008: The oscillations arise by the intersection between the lowest Landau tube and flat toroids on the Fermi surface induced by confinement.
elements.sent-2411-009: Positive and negative corrections to semi-classical magneto-conductance can occur by alternation between destructive and constructive interference in phase-coherent helical states.
elements.sent-2411-010: The quantum limit of Sondheimer oscillations emerges as another manifestation of Aharanov-Bohm flux quantization.
elements.sent-2411-011: Confinement of electrons to small spatial dimensions is known to affect electric transport properties \cite{Sondheimer1952,BRANDLI196961,Chambers1971,Triverdi1988 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: One size effect observed when the thickness of the sample becomes shorter than the bulk mean free path was discovered by Sondheimer \cite{Sondheimer1950 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: These are oscillations of conductivity, periodic in magnetic field, sometimes dubbed magneto-morphic.
elements.sent-2411-014: Common explanations of Sondheimer oscillations (SO) do not invoke Landau quantification.
elements.sent-2411-015: In contrast, Shubnikov-de Haas oscillations, which are periodic in the inverse of the magnetic field \cite{Shoenberg_1984 , are known to arise because of commensurability in momentum space between the Landau tubes and the extremal areas of the Fermi surface.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Sondheimer oscillations are understood to arise due to commensurability between the orbit of the charged carriers in real space and sample thickness.
elements.sent-2411-017: They are thus classified as a semi-classical phenomenon \cite{hurd2012hall .}

elements.sent-2411-018: Consider a magnetic field perpendicular to an electric current, generating an electric field (Fig. \ref{Fig.1 a).}
elements.sent-2411-019: Because of the Fermi velocity of mobile electrons, there is a Lorentz force on each electron, giving rise to a helical trajectory (Fig. \ref{Fig.1 b) whose axis is along the magnetic field.}
elements.sent-2411-020: With increasing magnetic field, the radius of each turn of the helix decreases.
elements.sent-2411-021: Concomitantly, the distance between these turns (i.e. the size of the helix pitch, $p$) is reduced.
elements.sent-2411-022: At particular values of magnetic field, separated by a fixed period, the thickness ($d$) of the sample becomes an integer number of helix pitches.
elements.sent-2411-023: In ballistic samples, where the mean-free-path limited by disorder is significantly longer than $d$, this leads to oscillations in conductivity, detected by numerous experiments in the second half of the last century \cite{Grenier-cd,Al-2,Al-3,CD-2,Cd-3,Cd-4Zn-1,Trodahl_1971,Sakamoto1976,Munarin1968 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: More recently, two studies have been devoted to Sondheimer oscillations in the Weyl semi-metal WP$_2$ \cite{van2021sondheimer and in graphite \cite{Taen .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The periodicity of the oscillations can be quantified thanks to a simple expression put forward by Harrison \cite{Harrison for velocity: \begin{equation}v_z = \frac{\hbar}{2\pi m^*}\frac{\partial A}{\partial k_{z}} \label{1}\end{equation} Here, $v_z$ and $k_z$ represent the Fermi velocity and the wavevector along the magnetic field.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The time for an electron to travel across the thickness is $\tau_1=d/v_z$.
elements.sent-2411-027: One revolution of the helix takes a time equal to $\tau_2= \frac{2\pi m^\star {eB}$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The $\tau_1/\tau_2$ ratio increases linearly with magnetic field and periodically becomes an integer.
elements.sent-2411-029: At these specific fields, the thickness is an integer number of helical revolutions.
elements.sent-2411-030: The period, $\Delta B$, is therefore given by \cite{Chambers1971,Grenier-cd,SONDER-1,hurd2012hall,van2021sondheimer : \begin{equation}\Delta B d = \frac{\hbar}{e} \frac{\partial A}{\partial k_{z}}. \label{2}\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-041: Here, we study longitudinal and Hall resistivity of cadmium single crystals with varying thicknesses ($d$), ranging from 12.6 to 475 $\mu$m.
elements.sent-2411-042: Employing focused ion beam (FIB) technology \cite{Moll2018 to etch the samples, we have ensured plane and parallel surfaces.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Our extensive study on five different samples with a forty-fold variation of thickness leads to solid conclusions regarding the period, the amplitude and the field dependence of oscillations.
elements.sent-2411-044: The period of oscillations, $\Delta B$, agrees with what was reported by Grenier et al. \cite{Grenier-cd for a thicker sample.}
elements.sent-2411-045: On the other hand, the amplitude of oscillations in thin samples, follows $\delta \sigma \propto B^{-2.5 e^{-B/3\Delta B}$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: This is a field dependence, which has never been proposed or observed before.
elements.sent-2411-047: Only when the samples become sufficiently thick, the field dependence becomes $\sigma \propto B^{-4}$ as previously reported.
elements.sent-2411-048: The crossover between the two regimes of field dependence occurs at $\sim 10$ oscillations.
elements.sent-2411-049: In section II, we present our extensive data and show in detail what leads to the empirical expression of $B^{-2.5 e^{-B/B_0}$ as the best fit to the amplitude of oscillations as a function of magnetic field.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Then, we show that, normalized by the square of the inverse of the thickness, all curves of conductivity oscillations collapse on top of each other, a forty-fold variation in thickness not withstanding.
elements.sent-2411-051: The result constitutes a rare case of conductivity quantization in three dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-052: In section III, we contrast our experimental observation of this distinct type of conductivity oscillations with the semi-classical picture and demonstrate that a quantum mechanical treatment is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-053: Recalling that the Larmor radius of semi-classical orbits can never fall below the magnetic length, we argue that a classical-quantum crossover in unavoidable for thin samples at low magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-054: In absence of a quantum theory, we sketch a rudimentary picture, in which confinement sculpts steps and flat toroids on the Fermi surface.
elements.sent-2411-055: Oscillations arise by successive intersections between the lowest Landau tube and the flat toroids.
elements.sent-2411-056: This picture, drawn in reciprocal space, leads to an expression for the period of oscillations identical to the semi-classical one.
elements.sent-2411-057: In contrast to the latter, however, it allows for constructive and destructive interferences between phase coherent helical states and therefore positive and negative corrections to semiclassical magnetoconductivity.
elements.sent-2411-058: We propose that the exponential prefactor is caused by quantum tunneling between the Fermi Sea and the helical states and identify a promising candidate as the relevant tunneling action.
elements.sent-2411-059: Our picture confirms Chambers' conjecture \cite{Chambers1971 regarding a role played by the phase of the electronic wave-function.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Most probably, this role is crucial in the quantum regime.
elements.sent-2411-061: A rigorous theoretical account of our results remains a challenge to theory.

(a) Finite thickness $d$ distorts a Fermi surface. elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{Fig-helices-v2.jpg} \caption{Helical states. (a) In the classical regime, the radius of each turn of the helical trajectory of electrons is equal to the Larmor radius. Therefore, it is different for different electrons as a function of their radial wave-vector. It is shorter for electrons closer to the pole (in brown) than those further (in red). Since this radius cannot become shorter than the magnetic length, this picture breaks down when $k_r \ell_B <1$. This is the quantum regime shown in the lower panels, b and c. (b) When the magnetic field is equal to a half-integer times the product of $\frac{\hbar}{e}$ and $k_d\partial A / \partial k_{z}$, the upward and the downward propagating helical states have the same phase at the start and the end of their trajectories. The interference is expected to be constructive. (c) When the magnetic field is equal to an integer time the product of $\frac{\hbar}{e}$ and $k_d\partial A / \partial k_{z}$, there is $\pi$ phase shift. elements.sent-2411-255: Cd single crystals were grown by the vapor-phase transport method.
elements.sent-2411-256: A quartz tube with appropriate amounts of starting material Cd (99.9997\%) was kept at the growth temperature for two weeks in a two-zone furnace.
elements.sent-2411-257: Shiny plate-like crystals were produced in a temperature gradient of 300 –200$^{\circ}$C.
elements.sent-2411-259: The production method followed previous works \cite{van2021sondheimer .}
elements.sent-2411-260: First, the crystallographic orientation of the bulk crystal was determined using X-ray diffraction.
elements.sent-2411-261: Subsequently, the crystal was securely affixed to a SEM stub in the desired orientation.
elements.sent-2411-262: The critical step was to employ a focused beam of Ga ions to meticulously carve a rectangular slab, a lamella, from the crystal.
elements.sent-2411-263: This process unfolds in three stages.
elements.sent-2411-264: Initially, a high current of 50 nA was used to create two gaps within the crystal.
elements.sent-2411-265: These gaps were separated by a section of the crystal, approximately $\sim$25 $\mu$m thick and 150 $\mu$m long.
elements.sent-2411-266: Following this, a smaller current of 15 nA was applied to smoothen the crystal section, resulting in moderately flat sidewalls.
elements.sent-2411-267: The lamella remained attached to the parent crystal through two beams at its top.
elements.sent-2411-268: Afterwards, with a precise 7 nA current both sides of the lamella were fine cut, ensuring parallel sidewalls and a high level of smoothness.
elements.sent-2411-269: The obtained flakes were then carefully transferred to an alumina substrate.
elements.sent-2411-270: It is crucial to note that a very thin layer of ``Araldite'' epoxy adhesive must be applied to the substrate beforehand.
elements.sent-2411-271: Once positioned atop the epoxy adhesive, capillary forces naturally shaped the epoxy around the lamella, extending smoothly to each of its top surfaces without covering them.
elements.sent-2411-272: After allowing the epoxy adhesive to dry naturally, the substrate was taken to a sputtering machine.
elements.sent-2411-273: There, a brief RF etching process was performed, depositing a 300 nm layer of Au onto the lamella.
elements.sent-2411-274: To further refine the device, the Scios 2 system was utilized.
elements.sent-2411-275: Initially, the Au layer covering the active part of the device was removed using an acceleration voltage of 5 kV and an ion current of 2 nA.
elements.sent-2411-276: Subsequently, the overall device shape, including the contact positions, was precisely cut out at 0.3 nA and 30 kV.
elements.sent-2411-277: Finally, the Au layer away from the device was severed, ensuring a current flow exclusively through the device.
elements.sent-2411-278: With these steps completed, the device become ready for measurement.

elements.sent-2411-452: The electrical conductivity of metallic crystals exhibits size effects when the electron mean free path exceeds the sample thickness.
elements.sent-2411-453: One such phenomenon, known as Sondheimer oscillations, was discovered decades ago.
elements.sent-2411-454: These oscillations, periodic in magnetic field, have been hitherto treated with no reference to Landau quantization.
elements.sent-2411-455: Here, we present a study of longitudinal and transverse conductivity in cadmium single crystals with thicknesses ranging from 12.6 to 475 $\mu$m, and demonstrate that the amplitude of the first ten oscillations is determined by the quantum of conductance and a length scale that depends on the sample thickness, the magnetic length and the Fermi surface geometry.
elements.sent-2411-456: We argue that this scaling is unexpected in semiclassical scenarios and it arises from the degeneracy of the momentum derivative of the cross-sectional area $A$ along the orientation of the magnetic field $\frac{\partial A {\partial k_z}$ in cadmium, which couples Landau quantization to the discretization of $k_z$ imposed by the finite sample thickness.}
elements.sent-2411-457: We show that the oscillating component of the conductivity is uniquely governed by fundamental constants and the ratio of two degeneracies, which acts as an inverted filling factor.
elements.sent-2411-458: Our conjecture is supported by the absence of such scaling in thin copper crystals.
elements.sent-2411-459: Confinement of electrons to small spatial dimensions is known to affect electrical transport properties \cite{Sondheimer1952,BRANDLI196961,Chambers1971,Triverdi1988 .}
elements.sent-2411-460: Sondheimer discovered one such size effect \cite{Sondheimer1950 , which occurs when the sample thickness becomes shorter than the bulk mean free path.}
elements.sent-2411-461: These oscillations of conductivity, sometimes dubbed magneto-morphic, are periodic in the magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-462: Available explanations of Sondheimer oscillations (SO) do not invoke Landau quantization, in contrast to Shubnikov-de Haas oscillations.
elements.sent-2411-463: The latter are periodic in the inverse of the magnetic field \cite{Shoenberg_1984 and are known to arise from commensurability in momentum space between the Landau tubes and the extremal areas of the Fermi surface.}
elements.sent-2411-464: The prevailing understanding of Sondheimer oscillations invokes commensurability between the orbits of the charged carriers in real space and the sample thickness\cite{hurd2012hall .}
elements.sent-2411-465: The phenomenon is classified as a semiclassical size effect, where neither the discrete energy levels introduced by the magnetic field nor those resulting from spatial confinement play a role.

elements.sent-2411-680: In summary, we showed that in thin single crystals of cadmium hosting ballistic electrons, Sondheimer (or magnetomorphic) oscillations of conductivity display a field dependence never encountered before.
elements.sent-2411-681: Their amplitude is set by the quantum of conductance and a length scale depending only on the thickness, the magnetic length, and the Fermi surface geometry.
elements.sent-2411-682: We argued that to explain this experimental observation, one needs to go beyond the available semiclassical theories and take into account the interplay between Landau tubes and the steps sculpted on the Fermi surface by spatial confinement.
elements.sent-2411-683: We then showed this allows us to understand the empirical scaling as a simple expression in which the key parameter is a dimensionless parameter set by the Fermi surface geometry.
